team.name: Jacksonville Jaguars
team.current_draft_position: No first-round pick in 2026 (traded to CLE). First selection is Round 2 (No. 56 overall, approximate).

gm.name: James Gladstone
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Director of Scouting Strategy / Director of College Scouting, Los Angeles Rams (2016–2024)
gm.confidence_notes: Only one draft as GM (2025). Profile heavily reliant on Rams scouting tree and Gladstone's stated philosophy. Will require significant upward revision after 2026 and 2027 drafts.

career_path.org: Clayton High School / St. John Vianney High School
career_path.role: Student Activities Director / Football Coach / Educator
Senior Assistant to GM / Player Personnel Coordinator
Director of Scouting (during Super Bowl LVI season)
Director of Scouting Strategy / Director of College Scouting

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Les Snead, Los Angeles Rams GM (2012–present)
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Snead tree, pure and unbroken. Gladstone's entire NFL career was with the Rams under Snead, from 2016 through the Super Bowl LVI championship and the subsequent roster rebuild. The Snead/McVay philosophy emphasizes: (1) willingness to trade premium picks for proven talent ('F*** them picks'), (2) film-first evaluation over combine testing, (3) finding late-round and Day 3 value through college production scouting, (4) not adhering to rigid physical profiles at positions — prioritizing toughness, work ethic and system fit over measurables, (5) using free agency to address needs so the draft can be used for BPA and depth, (6) remote combine evaluation rather than in-person attendance. Brad Holmes (Lions GM) is the most prominent other branch of this tree. Gladstone worked alongside Holmes and inherited the same evaluation DNA.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Gladstone has already demonstrated a willingness to diverge from the later-era Snead 'no first-round picks' approach. He traded UP in his first draft, aggressively moving from 5 to 2 for Travis Hunter — the first time a team traded into the top-2 for a non-QB since the Rams moved up for Orlando Pace in 1997. He explicitly acknowledged this break, saying 'I probably got some familiarity with the f*** them picks sort of process' but framed it as being bold rather than hoarding picks. This suggests Gladstone absorbed Snead's aggressiveness and fearlessness about trading picks, but will apply it in the opposite direction when he believes a player is transcendent. Also notably, Gladstone chose not to conduct any top-30 visits before the 2025 draft — citing implicit bias concerns and preferring 'sourced intel' from scouts — a deliberate methodological choice that goes beyond even the Rams' typical approach.
scouting_tree.cross_pollination: Minimal. Gladstone's entire front office career was with one organization (Rams). However, he now works with HC Liam Coen, who also has Rams roots (2018–2020, 2022) but also spent time with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers (2024) and Kentucky. The Rams influence is doubly reinforced through both the GM and HC connections.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Only 1 draft of own data. Lineage weight is extremely high. Gladstone's own tendencies are emerging (bold trade-up, emphasis on 'intangibly rich' players, high-character and high-production focus) but the Rams framework is the dominant predictive model. Weight should drop to 0.50 after the 2026 draft and 0.30 after 2027.

front_office.primary_decision_maker: James Gladstone, with significant collaborative input from HC Liam Coen and EVP Tony Boselli

key_influencers.name: Liam Coen
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded. Rams/McVay coaching tree — was Rams assistant 2018–2020 and 2022 OC. Tampa Bay OC 2024 (led NFL's only top-5 offense in every major category). Values QB development, tempo-based offense, pre-snap motion, creative personnel usage. Strong voice on offensive draft targets. Emphasizes 'toughness' and players who love football.

key_influencers.name: Tony Boselli
key_influencers.title: Executive Vice President of Football Operations
key_influencers.known_biases: Hall of Fame offensive tackle. Reports directly to owner Shad Khan. Provides 'football acumen and counsel' per Khan. As a former elite OL, likely has strong opinions on offensive line evaluation. No front-office scouting experience prior to this role — came from healthcare industry. His influence is more cultural/advisory than scouting-technical.

key_influencers.name: Shad Khan
key_influencers.title: Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: Structured the organization so that Gladstone, Coen, and Boselli all report directly to him. This creates potential for owner involvement in major decisions. Tony Khan (son) serves as Chief Football Strategy Officer and also has a role in football operations. Khan was willing to fire both HC and GM after 2024's 4-13 season — showing he will act when results disappoint. But his pivot to youth (youngest GM in NFL, young HC) suggests he's hands-off on day-to-day football decisions and trusts the people he hires.

key_influencers.name: Jake Tamme
key_influencers.title: Personnel/Analytics Collaborator (brought from Rams)
key_influencers.known_biases: Gladstone brought Tamme from the Rams to implement analytical systems. At the Rams, Tamme built the 'Joint After Action Review System.' This signals a data-driven, process-oriented approach to draft evaluation that complements the film-first Rams philosophy.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative three-pillar structure: Gladstone (personnel/scouting), Coen (coaching/scheme fit), Boselli (organizational/advisory). Gladstone has final say on draft picks but Coen clearly has significant influence on offensive targets and scheme fit. Both are young and were hired nearly simultaneously — creating a partnership dynamic rather than a hierarchy. Gladstone and Coen have overlapping Rams DNA which makes alignment easier.
front_office.owner_interference: low-to-medium — Khan structured the reporting lines to have all three leaders report to him, which is unusual. But early evidence suggests he's empowering the football people. Tony Khan's CFSO title is worth monitoring.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Bold, aggressive, willing to make moves for transcendent talent. Values 'intangibly rich' players who love football. Emphasized wanting to 'send messages about who the Jacksonville Jaguars are going to be.' Inherited Les Snead's macro philosophy of using free agency to fill needs so the draft can be used for best available talent.
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: In his only draft, Gladstone was heavily need/upside-driven in Round 1 (traded up massively for a player who filled two positions of need — WR and CB — in Travis Hunter). The mid-round picks addressed clear team needs: secondary (Ransaw R3), OL depth (Milum R3), RB (Tuten R4), LB (Kiser R4). The BPA score is moderated because several of these picks were consensus value picks at positions of need (Milum at 89 was PFF's 89th overall prospect). However, the boldness of the Round 1 move was clearly need-accelerated — the Jaguars had the NFL's worst pass defense and needed receivers.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Gladstone's mentor Les Snead has explicitly stated: 'Our macro philosophy is, if we can use free agency to not be desperate in the draft, we more than likely will be better drafters.' Gladstone's first draft shows the opposite approach — he made a desperate move to trade up for a player to fix two major needs simultaneously. This may reflect the unique roster situation (4-13 team needing a culture reset) rather than a permanent philosophy. The Rams influence on BPA/need balance will likely manifest more clearly once the Jaguars' roster is more competitive, which it now is after a 13-4 season.

trade_triggers: Transcendent talent available within range — will pay a premium for players he views as generational
Unique skill sets that fill multiple needs simultaneously (Hunter as WR/CB)
Day 3 targets he identified pre-draft that he's worried will be taken (mentioned Tuten, Kiser, Monheim were targets he presented during his GM interview)
Mid-round accumulation — traded down in Round 3 with Detroit to gain additional 2026 Day 2 picks
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Gladstone is a product of the Rams' 'F*** them picks' philosophy and has explicitly embraced it. He traded away the 2026 first-round pick plus a 2025 second and fourth to move up 3 spots. He also made multiple in-season trades (Christian Kirk for a 7th, Tank Bigsby for a 5th and 6th, Greg Newsome acquisition, Jakobi Meyers acquisition, Tim Patrick acquisition). He is clearly willing to mortgage future capital for immediate impact, but also showed savvy in accumulating mid-round picks via trades (gained two extra 2026 3rd-rounders from Detroit). The net posture is aggressive but not reckless — he's replacing quantity with targeted quality.

trade_history_detail.trade: Traded up from 5 to 2 with Cleveland
trade_history_detail.notes: Massive overpay by trade chart standards. Gladstone said framework was in place 2 weeks before draft. Called it 'a stretch' he thought wouldn't work. Reflects Snead-level boldness.

trade_history_detail.trade: Traded down in Round 3 with Detroit
trade_history_detail.notes: Classic Rams-style trade-back to accumulate Day 2 picks. Moved down 18 spots in R3 but got an extra 3rd this year plus two future 3rds.

position_avoidance: LB appears deprioritized in early rounds — consistent with Rams tree. As noted: 'The team has heavy roots in the Rams' front office philosophy and Los Angeles has put minimal investment into the position.' Both LBs drafted were Day 3 picks (Kiser R4, McLeod R6).
TE was not addressed in the 2025 draft at all. Consistent with Rams' recent drafting patterns.
position_premium: WR/CB — the Travis Hunter pick fundamentally demonstrates that Gladstone values playmakers and coverage players above all else when transcendent talent is available
Versatile defensive backs — Ransaw (R3) was described as a 'versatile' DB with slot and outside experience; Hunter is the ultimate versatile DB/WR
OL depth — Gladstone explicitly said 'You can never have enough security across the offensive line' and drafted Milum (OT, R3) and Monheim (C, R7) in his first draft
positional_value.notes: The Rams tree values positional versatility extremely highly. Gladstone's first draft reflects this: Hunter (WR/CB), Ransaw (versatile DB), Kiser (LB who played S at Senior Bowl), McLeod (LB/EDGE hybrid). Players who can play multiple positions appear to get a significant boost on his board.

WR_CB_hybrid.archetype_summary: Gladstone's signature pick is Travis Hunter — a two-way WR/CB who is a generational outlier. This tells us Gladstone values versatility above all, and is willing to pay any price for a player he views as transcendent. For non-unicorn picks, the Rams scouting tree is the guide: Cooper Kupp (R3, 2017) and Puka Nacua (R5, 2023) were drafted during Gladstone's tenure — both are high-production, route-running-first receivers who may lack elite speed but compensate with ball skills, competitive toughness, and football IQ.

physical_profile.height_range: No rigid requirement — Hunter is 6-1, Kupp is 6-1, Nacua is 6-2. The Rams/Gladstone tree has shown no strong size preference at WR.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Film-first evaluation. The Rams have famously not attended the combine in person since 2019, and Gladstone adopted this approach for 2026. Athletic testing data is reviewed remotely as a data point, not a deciding factor.

most_valued_traits: Competitive toughness / willingness to play through contact
Positional versatility (can play inside and outside)
least_important_traits: Pure top-end speed — Kupp ran 4.62, Nacua ran 4.43. Neither was a 4.3 burner. Film and production trump speed.
deal_breakers: Lack of production — Kupp had 117 catches his final college season; Nacua had 64 catches. Both were prolific producers.
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Unique versatility (Hunter) or elite ball skills paired with high production

college_production.production_floor: Hunter: 96 catches, 15 TDs, 4 INTs in 2024. Both Kupp and Nacua were top producers at their college programs. The Rams tree consistently drafts productive college WRs.
college_production.breakout_age_sensitivity: Medium — both Kupp (4-year starter) and Nacua (late bloomer at BYU before transferring) show the tree values production over breakout timing

examples: 2025 1.2 — Travis Hunter: WR/CB, 6-1, 188 lbs. 96 catches, 1,258 yards, 15 TDs + 4 INTs in 2024. Heisman winner. Traded up 3 spots to select.

WR_CB_hybrid.confidence: low — only 1 WR drafted by Gladstone. Rams tree (Kupp, Nacua, Tutu Atwell, Van Jefferson) provides supplementary signal.

CB.archetype_summary: Versatile defensive backs who can play multiple coverage roles. The 2025 draft shows a clear preference for versatile, ball-hawking, competitive DBs over pure press-man specialists. Campanile's defense is predominantly zone-based, so coverage versatility and ball skills matter more than pure press technique. Hunter and Ransaw both fit this mold — players who can play multiple alignments and have instincts for the football.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 to 6-1 based on Hunter (6-1) and Ransaw. The Rams tree has drafted corners across the size spectrum — no rigid height minimum.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: The Rams have shown willingness to draft corners who don't fit traditional NFL size/speed thresholds. Gladstone's 2025 class had high RAS scores overall (Ransaw: 9.75 RAS), suggesting athleticism is valued but not in a rigid measurables-first way.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Zone-based with multiplicity. Campanile's 2025 Jaguars were predominantly zone. The scheme values 'vision on the quarterback' to create takeaways rather than man-coverage lockdown.
most_valued_traits: Competitive toughness at point of attack
least_important_traits: Pure press-man technique — scheme doesn't demand it as the primary skill

college_production.production_floor: Hunter had 4 INTs in 2024, Ransaw was described as a 'fast, do-everything type.' The emphasis on takeaways (31 turnovers in 2025 under Campanile, up from 9 in 2024) suggests they value DBs who create turnovers.

examples: 2025 1.2 — Travis Hunter: 6-1, 188 lbs. 7 career INTs at Colorado. Ball skills among the best in 25 years per NFL Media.
2025 3.88 — Caleb Ransaw, Tulane: Versatile DB, 9.75 RAS. Described as 'fast, do-everything type.' Missed rookie season due to injury.

CB.confidence: low-medium — 2 DBs drafted, scheme context from Campanile helps

EDGE.archetype_summary: Based on Rams tree and one Day 3 pick (McLeod): Hybrid EDGE/LB players who can rush the passer and play off-ball. The Rams under Gladstone's scouting tenure drafted Jared Verse (2024 R1 — DROY), Byron Young (2023 R3), and other productive pass rushers. The common thread across the Rams tree is production over prototype — they draft players who get to the QB regardless of size or 'fit.' Gladstone's pick of McLeod (EDGE who also played LB at Auburn and the Senior Bowl) reinforces the versatility emphasis.

physical_profile.height_range: Flexible — the Rams drafted 6-1 Josaiah Stewart as an EDGE in 2025. No rigid length requirement.
physical_profile.weight_range: Flexible — tree has drafted EDGEs from 240–270+
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Film and production drive the evaluation. The Rams' identity is 'they don't adhere to any hard and fast rules when it comes to physical profiles.'

most_valued_traits: Production — sack totals and disruption plays matter
least_important_traits: Size/length thresholds — explicitly not a factor per Rams philosophy

college_production.production_floor: McLeod had 22.5 career sacks (8 in final season). Verse had 17 sacks in 2 years at FSU. The tree values production.
college_production.notes: Rams' 2023 picks (Byron Young, Kobie Turner) came from non-traditional programs and were late-round steals. Gladstone is credited with finding production in unexpected places.

examples: 2025 6.194 — Jalen McLeod, Auburn: EDGE/LB hybrid. 22.5 career sacks. Willingly played out of position at Senior Bowl.
rams_tree_supplement: 2024 1.19 — Jared Verse: 6-4, 247. 17 sacks in 2 years at FSU. DROY. Gladstone was Director of College Scouting.
2023 3.77 — Byron Young: Sack production from Tennessee. 3rd-round steal.
2023 3.89 — Kobie Turner: DL from Wake Forest. Led rookies in sacks.

EDGE.confidence: low for JAX-specific data. Medium-high when Rams scouting tree is factored.

OT.archetype_summary: The Rams tree values offensive linemen who can operate in a zone-blocking scheme with versatility to play multiple positions. Gladstone explicitly prioritized OL depth, saying 'You can never have enough security across the offensive line.' Milum (R3) was a four-year starter at West Virginia who projects inside to guard, showing the tree values versatile OL who can play both tackle and guard. The prior Baalke regime drafted Anton Harrison (2023 R1, OT Oklahoma) — he remains a starter under Gladstone.

physical_profile.notes: Milum was described as having great 'balance and strong hands.' 9.16 RAS. The Rams have drafted OL Rob Havenstein and Joe Noteboom — favoring technically sound linemen over pure physical freaks.

most_valued_traits: Versatility — ability to play inside and outside
least_important_traits: Pure mauling run blocking at the expense of athleticism

examples: 2025 3.89 — Wyatt Milum, West Virginia: OT, 4-year starter. Big 12 OL of the Year 2024. 9.16 RAS. Projects as guard in NFL.

IOL.archetype_summary: Depth-focused, technical interior linemen. Gladstone drafted Jonah Monheim (C, USC) in the 7th round and explicitly presented him as a Day 3 target during his GM interview. This suggests he values center-capable interior OL as late-round depth pieces, consistent with the Rams' approach of building OL depth through the draft at all levels.

examples: 2025 7.221 — Jonah Monheim, USC: Center. 3rd-team All-Big Ten. Gladstone identified him pre-draft as a target.

DT.archetype_summary: The Jaguars did NOT draft any defensive tackles in 2025 despite clear need (had 25th-ranked run defense in 2024). This is notable and may reflect two things: (1) Gladstone may have believed the veteran DTs (Armstead, Hamilton) were adequate and the defense's issues were schematic, not personnel; (2) The Rams tree has historically drafted undersized, disruptive interior linemen — all five DTs drafted by the Rams recently were under 300 lbs. DT is identified as a major 2026 need with Armstead aging (33). Expect Gladstone to invest heavily here in the 2026 draft.
DT.notes: Campanile used Travon Walker as an interior pass-rusher in 2025, suggesting the scheme values versatile interior disruption over traditional two-gap nose tackles.

evidence.rams_tree_supplement: Rams have drafted DTs under 300 lbs consistently. Kobie Turner (2023 R3) was an undersized, disruptive 3-tech.

DT.confidence: very_low — no picks. Must rely on Rams tree and 2026 draft to build profile.

S.archetype_summary: Versatile, multi-role defensive backs rather than traditional single-high or box safeties. Ransaw (drafted as 'defensive back' not specifically 'safety' or 'corner') and Rayuan Lane III (Navy, R6) fit a pattern of versatile defensive backs who can wear multiple hats. Lane was described as a 'special teams warrior' who played safety with 244 career tackles, 21 PBUs, and 7 INTs — a productive, tough, high-character player from a service academy.

examples: 2025 3.88 — Caleb Ransaw, Tulane: Listed as DB with versatility across safety and corner positions. 9.75 RAS.
2025 6.200 — Rayuan Lane III, Navy: Safety. 244 career tackles, 7 INTs. Special teams contributor. Service academy pedigree (high character).

LB.archetype_summary: High-character, productive, versatile linebackers who love football and will play out of position willingly. Both LBs drafted (Kiser R4, McLeod R6) were highlighted for their willingness to play positions outside their comfort zone at the Senior Bowl. Kiser played safety there despite being exclusively a linebacker at Notre Dame; McLeod played off-ball despite being an on-ball/EDGE player at Auburn. This is the clearest expression of Gladstone's 'intangibly rich' philosophy — he wants competitors who embrace versatility. Production matters: Kiser had 13.5 TFLs and 8 sacks in his final season; McLeod had 22.5 career sacks.

physical_profile.notes: No rigid size requirements. Kiser was a 70-game veteran at Notre Dame. Both players valued for football character over physical traits.

most_valued_traits: Versatility — ability to play multiple positions
least_important_traits: Pure athleticism over character — Kiser had only a 7.68 RAS but was an 'intangibly rich' pick

examples: 2025 4.107 — Jack Kiser, Notre Dame: LB. 70 games played (school record). Team captain. Notre Dame Man of the Year. 13.5 TFLs, 8 sacks in 2024. Played safety at Senior Bowl. Campbell/Wuerffel Trophy finalist.
2025 6.194 — Jalen McLeod, Auburn: EDGE/LB. 22.5 career sacks. Played off-ball at Senior Bowl willingly. 'Didn't bat an eye.'

LB.confidence: medium — 2 picks with clear archetype pattern

RB.archetype_summary: Explosive, multi-dimensional backs who offer value beyond traditional rushing. Tuten (R4) was the fastest RB at the 2025 combine (9.55 RAS) and was a kick return specialist at Virginia Tech. Allen (R7) led all FBS RBs in receptions (64 in 2024) and was a true dual-threat. Both were high-production college backs. The Coen offensive scheme demands versatile backs who can catch and contribute in the passing game — consistent with the Rams tree (Todd Gurley, Cam Akers, Kyren Williams).

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters — Tuten was the fastest RB at the combine. But receiving ability and versatility matter more than pure size.
physical_profile.notes: Gladstone identified Tuten as a 'priority target' on Day 3 during his GM interview, suggesting he scouts for specific traits (speed + versatility) at the position.

most_valued_traits: Kick return value / special teams contribution
least_important_traits: Pure power/between-the-tackles rushing — neither Tuten nor Allen is a traditional thumper

college_production.notes: Tuten: 2,963 all-purpose yards in final 2 seasons, 2 KR TDs. Allen: led FBS RBs with 64 catches in 2024, 1,021 rushing yards. Both were prolific multi-year producers.

examples: 2025 4.104 — Bhayshul Tuten, Virginia Tech: RB. Fastest RB at combine. 9.55 RAS. 2,963 all-purpose yards in 2 seasons. Kick returner. Gladstone pre-identified as Day 3 target.
2025 7.236 — LeQuint Allen Jr., Syracuse: RB. 64 receptions in 2024 (led FBS RBs). 1,021 rushing yards, 16 TDs. Dual-threat.

TE.archetype_summary: No TEs drafted in the 2025 draft. The Jaguars used Parker Washington (converted WR) and Brenton Strange at TE in 2025. The Rams tree has drafted TEs sparingly — Tyler Higbee was the most notable. Coen's scheme uses TEs as H-backs and flex players rather than traditional inline blockers. Expect any future TE draft pick to be a receiving-first, athletic profile.

QB.archetype_summary: No QBs drafted — the Jaguars have Trevor Lawrence locked in. The Rams tree drafted Jared Goff (1st overall, 2016) during Gladstone's tenure — a pocket passer with accuracy and processing ability. Lawrence fits this mold. Gladstone's offensive scheme under Coen demands QBs who can 'read defenses, make quick decisions, and deliver accurate passes on the run.' No data on developmental QB preferences.

reach_tolerance.average_reach_picks: 15+ in Round 1 (massive reach by trade chart standards for Hunter, though Hunter was PFF's #1 overall prospect)
reach_tolerance.max_observed_reach: Hunter trade-up was not a 'reach' on the player — he was consensus top-2. The reach was in the trade cost, not the player selection.
reach_tolerance.round_1_reach_tolerance: Willing to overpay dramatically in draft capital to secure the player he wants. Not afraid to 'reach' in terms of trade value.
reach_tolerance.mid_round_notes: Ransaw at 88 was called 'a little early' by some analysts (Kiper). Milum at 89 matched Kiper's ranking exactly. Tuten at 104 was considered value. Overall approach: slight willingness to reach for scheme/character fits in mid-rounds.
reach_tolerance.notes: Gladstone's reach tolerance is unusual — he's not reaching on players versus consensus rankings, but he's willing to dramatically overpay in draft capital to secure specific targets. The Snead influence is evident: value isn't about getting the cheapest price, it's about getting the right player.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: McVay/Coen-influenced offense: spread formations, RPOs, heavy pre-snap motion, tempo-based, play-action driven. Emphasizes QB development and mismatch creation. Zone-run concepts with creative scheme designs (Wildcat, fake screens, misdirection). In 2025 under Coen, the Jaguars ranked 6th in scoring (27.9 ppg). The offense demands versatile skill players, athletic OL who can pull and zone-block, and receiving-capable RBs and TEs.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Campanile's defense is a multiple scheme — described 'as a 4-3 with some 3-4 spacing' but best classified as 'multiple.' Predominantly zone-based coverage with aggressive blitzing. 277 blitzes in 2025, 163 QB pressures, 22 INTs. Emphasis on 'violence at the point of attack,' run-and-hit style, takeaway creation. Values versatile defenders who can play multiple roles. Ranked #1 in run defense and #2 in takeaways in 2025.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: Both OC Grant Udinski and DC Anthony Campanile agreed to return for 2026 after interviewing for HC jobs elsewhere. Scheme continuity is locked in.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Very aggressive. Made the biggest trade-up for a non-QB since 1997 in his FIRST draft. Inherited the 'F*** them picks' mindset from Snead. Also made multiple in-season trades (Meyers, Patrick, Newsome, Kirk, Bigsby). Gladstone explicitly said: 'By being hand in hand with Les, it was fairly clear that he was somebody that didn't operate out of fear. That's something that I've held onto.' This is a high-risk, high-conviction GM.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate-to-high. His first draft directly addressed the Jaguars' 2024 catastrophic failures: worst pass defense in NFL history → drafted Travis Hunter (WR/CB) and Caleb Ransaw (DB). Worst run defense → not directly addressed in draft but scheme change under Campanile fixed it. As the team's needs evolve post-13-4 season, we'll see if his 2026 draft reflects the 2025 season's shortcomings (DT depth, safety, LB if Lloyd leaves).
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Gladstone showed zero attachment to previous regime's players. He cut Devin Duvernay, Evan Engram, and Gabe Davis immediately. Parted ways with assistant GM Ethan Waugh after the draft. Traded previous draft picks (Kirk, Bigsby, Campbell) for future capital. He operates like a new-regime GM who treats inherited players as assets to evaluate, not commitments to honor.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Film-first, consistent with Rams tree. The Rams haven't attended the combine in person since 2019. Gladstone and Coen skipped the 2026 combine in person, citing the Rams' approach. Gladstone said all data is 'available remotely.' His 2025 draft class did have high average RAS scores (Rams have 6th-most 8.0+ RAS picks in recent years), but this appears to be a byproduct of valuing athletic production, not a measurables-first approach. He values athleticism as confirmed by film, not as standalone data.
psychological_profile.character_description: Extremely high. 'Intangibly rich' is Gladstone's foundational draft philosophy. He explicitly stated: 'These guys love ball, and that's what we're hunting up.' Every pick in 2025 was described in terms of character: Hunter (generational competitor), Kiser (Notre Dame Man of the Year, 70-game career, Campbell Trophy finalist), McLeod (willingly played out of position at Senior Bowl), Lane (Navy — military academy pedigree). Gladstone chose not to conduct top-30 visits, saying 'sourced intel from those who have lived with these individuals is likely to be more accurate than me sitting down with a prospect for a short period of time.' He also described his approach to implicit bias: 'I don't view myself as having this extreme superpower of deciphering the complexity of a person in an hour.' This is a character-first GM.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Has Trevor Lawrence under contract. Lawrence was an MVP finalist in 2025 under Coen's system. No developmental QB was drafted in 2025. Gladstone appears committed to Lawrence as the franchise QB. The Rams tree traded for Matthew Stafford rather than developing a QB, suggesting a preference for established QB play. May draft a developmental backup in later rounds but no evidence of QB dissatisfaction.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Strongly leans toward production. Every player in the 2025 draft was a prolific college producer: Hunter (Heisman winner, 96 catches + 4 INTs), Milum (4-year starter, Big 12 OL of the Year), Kiser (70 games played, 8 sacks), Tuten (fastest RB at combine + 2,963 all-purpose yards), Allen (64 receptions, led FBS RBs). Austen Lane of Action News JAX explicitly identified 'Production Score' as the key metric for predicting Gladstone's picks, calling it 'his ethos, his religion.' The Rams under Gladstone's scouting also drafted high-production players: Cooper Kupp (117 catches), Puka Nacua (established records). This is a production-first evaluator.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: Hunter: 96 catches, 15 TDs in final season. Kupp: 117 catches at EWU. Nacua: 64 catches as a 5th-round steal. High production is near-mandatory for early-round WRs.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: Tuten: 2,963 all-purpose yards in 2 seasons. Allen: 1,021 rushing yards + 64 catches. Multi-dimensional production valued.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: Kiser: 90 tackles, 13.5 TFLs, 8 sacks. McLeod: 22.5 career sacks. Production is a clear driver.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: Based on Rams tree — Verse had 17 sacks in 2 years. Young and Turner were productive mid-round picks. Minimum sack production appears required.
production_thresholds_by_position.OL: Milum: 4-year starter, conference awards. Multi-year starting experience is valued.

college_production_preferences.notes: The emphasis on 'intangibly rich' players who 'love ball' maps directly to college production — players who love football tend to produce heavily. Gladstone doesn't appear to separate character evaluation from production evaluation; they're intertwined. A player with elite traits but mediocre production would likely be viewed skeptically because the production gap suggests something is missing intangibly.

WR.notes: Hunter: 6-1, 188. Not a speed demon. Rams tree drafted Kupp at 4.62 40. Film and production over measurables.

CB.notes: Ransaw: 9.75 RAS (elite). Hunter: elite ball skills compensate for lacking top-end speed. Athleticism valued but not rigidly threshold-based.

EDGE.notes: No JAX-specific data. Rams drafted 6-1 EDGE (Stewart). No height/weight minimums.

OT.notes: Milum: 9.16 RAS (very high). Athletic OL valued.

RB.notes: Tuten was fastest RB at combine. 9.55 RAS. Speed is clearly valued at RB.

LB.notes: Kiser: 7.68 RAS (below average). Character and production trumped athleticism here.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Soft preferences at best. The Rams/Gladstone tree is defined by NOT having hard cutoffs. As analyzed: 'There's one hard and fast rule when it comes to the Rams draft philosophy — they don't adhere to any hard and fast rules when it comes to physical profiles.'

size_preferences_by_position.DT: Rams have drafted all DTs under 300 lbs in recent years. Expect similar approach.
size_preferences_by_position.LB: No strong size preference based on Kiser/McLeod
size_preferences_by_position.S: No data — Lane from Navy was a multi-year starter

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Overall RAS / athleticism score appears valued as a tiebreaker but never as a primary driver. The 2025 class had high average athleticism (Ransaw 9.75, Milum 9.16, Tuten 9.55) but Kiser's 7.68 shows character can override athleticism. Gladstone reviews combine data remotely — it's a data input, not a decision driver.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film wins, always. This is the defining characteristic of the Rams scouting tree. Gladstone explicitly chose not to attend the combine in person. He trusts 'sourced intel' over direct observation. The Rams' approach: 'Film-first evaluation — game tape outweighs workout performance. Controlled data review — measurements and testing results are analyzed without distraction.'

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Go big or go home. Gladstone's only R1 pick was a trade-up for a generational talent. He views Round 1 as the place to acquire franchise-altering players, not safe depth. If no transcendent talent is available, the Rams tree suggests he would happily trade back and accumulate mid-round picks. In 2026, the Jaguars don't have a 1st-round pick (traded to CLE), so this dimension won't apply.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: The sweet spot. Gladstone's Rams traded their R3 pick down and gained additional mid-round capital. His R3 picks (Ransaw, Milum) were targeted need-fills with value alignment. The Rams are historically elite in this range (Kupp R3, Young R3, Turner R3, Nacua R5). Expect Gladstone to prioritize Day 2 as the core of his draft strategy in 2026, especially since it will be his highest picks available.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Pre-identified targets with specific roles. Gladstone presented Tuten, Kiser, and Monheim as Day 3 targets during his GM interview — before he was even hired. This suggests detailed advance scouting and specific role projections for Day 3 picks. Day 3 is for: (1) speed/athletic upside players who fell (Tuten), (2) high-character veterans who provide depth (Kiser — 70 games played), (3) special teams contributors (Lane — Navy gunner), (4) OL depth (Monheim). The Rams have been elite at Day 3 scouting (Nacua R5 set rookie records).
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: No data yet — insufficient track record.

hit_rate_by_position.notes: Only 1 draft to evaluate. Hunter played 7 games before knee injury (28 catches, 298 yards, 1 TD on offense; 15 tackles, 3 PBUs on defense in limited snaps). Tuten had immediate impact as backup RB and kick returner. Kiser started 16 games and led team with 90 tackles. Milum contributed on OL. Ransaw and McLeod missed their rookie seasons with injuries. Monheim contributed as backup C. Allen had limited role. Lane had special teams role.
hit_rate_by_position.early_assessment: Kiser appears to be a clear hit (4th-round LB who became a starter). Tuten showed promise. Hunter's career is too early and injury-impacted to assess. The injury attrition to Ransaw and McLeod prevents evaluation of those picks.

blind_spots: No DT addressed despite clear 2024 need — suggests either philosophical deprioritization or belief that scheme changes would fix the run defense (which they did). May be a blind spot if DT evaluation proves weak in future drafts.
strengths: Day 3 value identification — Kiser at R4 becoming a starter is a strong early signal. Consistent with Rams tree's strength in mid-to-late-round evaluation.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Moderate strategic spender. In his first offseason (2025), Gladstone signed Robert Hainsey, Chuma Edoga, Dyami Brown, Hunter Long, Nick Mullens, Johnny Mundt, Jourdan Lewis, and Eric Murray — mostly veteran stopgaps and depth pieces. He also made significant in-season trades (Jakobi Meyers from LV, Greg Newsome from CLE, Tim Patrick). This mirrors Snead's philosophy of using FA to 'not be desperate in the draft.' The Jaguars' 2025 FA moves filled immediate holes so the draft could be used for high-upside players.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When he signed veteran DBs (Lewis, Murray) in FA, he still drafted Hunter and Ransaw — suggesting he doesn't stop drafting a position just because FA addressed it. He views FA as establishing a baseline and the draft as the upside play.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions, supplement through FA and trades. In-season trades are a key tool — he made 5+ transactions during the 2025 season. The Rams model of aggressive asset management (trading underperforming players for picks, acquiring immediate-impact veterans) is clearly in effect.

notable_patterns: Traded UP in Round 1 for a generational two-way player — unprecedented boldness for a first-time GM
Traded DOWN in Round 3 to accumulate Day 2 capital — balancing aggression with value accumulation
Every pick emphasized character, leadership, and college production over pure athleticism
Class included multiple team captains, award winners, and players with 50+ games of college experience
Drafted 4 defensive players, 4 offensive players, plus Hunter (both) — balanced approach
Did not conduct any top-30 visits — relying entirely on 'sourced intel' and film evaluation
Pre-identified specific Day 3 targets during his GM interview months before the draft — detailed advance preparation
RAS scores averaged very high across the class, but this was a byproduct of targeting productive athletes, not measurables-first scouting

2025_draft_class.notes: Traded up from 5. Heisman winner. Cost: 2025 2nd, 2025 4th, 2026 1st.
Versatile DB. 9.75 RAS. Traded into spot via Detroit deal.
4-year starter. Big 12 OL of the Year. 9.16 RAS. Projects to guard.
Fastest RB at combine. 9.55 RAS. Pre-identified target. Received via CLE trade.
70 games played. Team captain. Man of the Year. Played S at Senior Bowl. Pre-identified target.
22.5 career sacks. Hybrid LB/EDGE. Played off-ball at Senior Bowl willingly.
244 career tackles, 7 INTs. Special teams value. Military academy pedigree.
OL depth. Pre-identified target from GM interview.
64 receptions in 2024 (led FBS RBs). Dual-threat back.

biggest_reaches.consensus_rank: 1-2 (not a player reach — a trade capital reach)
biggest_reaches.reach_amount: Trade cost was dramatic overpay by standard trade value charts — surplus value equivalent of a top-10 pick went to Cleveland
biggest_reaches.context: New GM making a statement pick. Jaguars had worst pass defense in franchise history in 2024. Hunter was PFF's #1 overall player.

biggest_steals.consensus_rank: Considered a steal by most analysts — fastest RB at combine falling to Day 3
biggest_steals.context: Gladstone pre-identified Tuten as a Day 3 target during his GM interview in February. Was worried he wouldn't be available after his combine performance.
Pre-identified target. Became a starter with 90 tackles. 'More fundamentally sound than many professionals' per CBS.

picks_owned: Round 3 (from Detroit — second pick from R3 trade-back)
key_needs_entering_2026_draft: DT — Armstead aging (33), Hamilton inconsistent, no depth behind them. #1 need. Multiple DTs being mocked (Christen Miller, A'Mauri Washington, Kayden McDonald).
CB — Multiple starters (Montaric Brown, Greg Newsome II, Christian Braswell) hitting free agency. Hunter shifting to more CB snaps helps but depth is thin.
LB — Devin Lloyd (2nd-team All-Pro) expected to leave in free agency. Foyesade Oluokun potential cap casualty.
IOL — Robert Hainsey and Patrick Mekari were veteran stopgaps. Need long-term interior OL solutions.
S — Andrew Wingard and Eric Murray are aging veterans who may depart.
RB — Travis Etienne is a free agent. Tuten and Allen are under contract but unproven as starters.
WR — Hunter shifting to defense creates a receiving void. Brian Thomas Jr. had inconsistent 2025. Jakobi Meyers is reliable but aging.
2026_draft_context.cap_situation: Projected -$15.7M over 2026 cap. Must make cuts/restructures before free agency.
2026_draft_context.roster_context: Coming off a 13-4 season and AFC South championship. Lost in Wild Card round to Buffalo 27-24. The team is in a 'win-now but build sustainably' phase. No 1st-round pick means mid-round value identification is critical. Gladstone must prove the Rams' Day 2-3 scouting prowess translates to Jacksonville.

confidence_notes: Only 1 draft as Jaguars GM. James Gladstone was hired February 2025 and has overseen one draft cycle and one regular season. The Rams scouting tree (Les Snead, 2016–2024) provides the primary behavioral baseline. Gladstone's stated philosophy ('intangibly rich,' bold, aggressive, production-focused, character-first, film-over-measurables) is consistent with his Rams background and his first draft's picks. However, one draft is insufficient to establish reliable patterns at the position-archetype level. The 2026 draft — where Gladstone will have 10+ mid-round picks and no R1 pick — will be the true test of whether he can replicate the Rams' legendary Day 2-3 scouting. Profile should be substantially updated after the 2026 draft.
